source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Initiative
CONTENT:
An Initiative represents a targeted, time-bound effort that drives change within the organization. It requires dedicated resources, impacts the enterprise architecture, and contributes directly to the achievement of one or more defined objectives. Initiatives serve as the operational link between strategic intent and execution, enabling measurable progress toward business goals.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Technical Implementation of Interfaces
CONTENT:
Interfaces are not always implemented directly by the IT Product that provides or consumes them. In many cases, middleware platforms, API gateways, or integration services handle the technical implementation. This separation between logical ownership and technical realization is essential for accurate modeling and architecture governance. Modeling Guidelines: • The logical interface is modeled between the provider and consumer IT Pro

SECTION_TITLE: Interfaces to External Systems
CONTENT:
In enterprise architecture, it is common for IT Products to interact with external systems— for example, to exchange data with banks, government agencies, or third-party services. These systems are considered external if they: • Are not part of the Porsche network (including ToGe networks), • Are not operated by PAG, a subsidiary, or a group brand, • Are not part of the internal Porsche IT landscape. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additi
subject: Lifecycle_Data

• If the product is part of an Agile Release Train (ART), link it to the corresponding Digital Solution.
• If the required Digital Solution is missing, request it from the responsible Program Management team.
• Always set the Support Type to indicate how the product supports the capability.
• Manual linking is not allowed.
subject: Digital_Solution

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Organization
CONTENT:
Organizations represent the users/owners of Applications or other business objetcts that can be modeled within different dimensions to create hierarchical structures. In Porsche AG's LeanIX, we distinguish between the elements of the structural organization and the elements of the process organization according to SAFe. The elements of the organizational structure represent the organizational structure of Porsche AG and its subsidiaries. The proce
subject: Organization

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Vendor / Provider
CONTENT:
A Vendor (referred to as Provider in the original LeanIX metamodel) represents an external organization responsible for supplying or manufacturing an IT component.
subject: Vendor

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Responsibility for IT Components
CONTENT:
The definition of who is responsible for an IT component continues to lead to heated discussions in the company. Especially due to the establishment of the so-called departmental IT (P51), we are in a dilemma here from an IT point of view. The person specified on a component is typically the person responsible for that component for the entire organization in terms of operations, maintenance, security, and compliance. subject: IT_Component

SECTION_TITLE: Creating a new IT component
CONTENT:
When a product, tool, or software is identified as a technical IT component, it must be modeled using an IT Component Fact Sheet. Required attributes and relations: • Category • Version (major only) • Description (in English) • Lifecycle • Vendor assignment

CONTENT:
• Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers
subject: Accurate_Lifecycle_Data

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: IT Product
CONTENT:
At Porsche, an IT Product is classified as either an Application or a Platform. Regardless of category, each IT Product must have clearly defined ownership and accountability. Applikationen An Application is an IT Product that delivers specific business functionality to support business processes—for example, by automating process steps. Applications contribute to the realization of business capabilities and are aligned with the value creation proce

• Domain Architects and Enterprise Architects can leverage it to optimize the IT portfolio, reduce duplication, and identify gaps in the technology landscape.

CONTENT:

It enables stakeholders to assess how an initiative affects key architectural domains, including: • Business Capabilities • Business Contexts • Applications • Organizations • Platforms • IT Components

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Business Process
CONTENT:
A business process is a structured sequence of logically connected activities designed to achieve a specific business or operational goal. A process may be: • Part of a higher-level process (process hierarchy), • Composed of subprocesses, • Or used to implement other processes.
subject: Business_Process

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
• A Digital Solution delivers end-to-end value to internal or external customers.
• Customers do not purchase isolated features or capabilities—they seek complete solutions that fulfill their desired outcomes.
• Digital Solutions enable organizations to align technical delivery with business strategy and customer needs.
subject: Organization_Alignment

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Governance and Ownership
CONTENT:
• There is no central authority for managing Objectives.
• Responsibility for creation, maintenance, and usage lies with the creator and user.
• For guidance, contact the Enterprise Architects of the respective portfolio or the Chief Architects.
1. Objective (pink) – Represents a strategic goal.
2. Initiative (green) – A time-bound effort to implement a goal.
